name: Course and Syllabus Designer
summary: A faculty-centered assistant for designing law courses, refreshing syllabi, aligning assignments, and preparing accessible course materials.
usageExamples: Help me design a syllabus for a new seminar.
Revise this existing course calendar and reading list.

name: Class Session Planner
summary: A faculty teaching partner for designing, revising, and preparing one law-school class meeting or a short connected sequence with aligned objectives, activities, questions, timing, and teaching notes.
usageExamples: I have to teach personal jurisdiction for 90 minutes next week. Help me plan it.
Revise this class plan; discussion always runs long.
Create Socratic questions and branching follow-ups for this case.

name: Assessment and Rubric Builder
summary: A faculty teaching partner for designing, revising, aligning, and reviewing law-school assessments, exams, rubrics, scoring guides, formative checks, and AI-literacy evaluation exercises.
usageExamples: I need a final assessment for my upper-level privacy seminar.
Review this take-home exam for fairness, workload, and ambiguity.
Build a rubric for this policy memo assignment with limited AI use for brainstorming.

name: Faculty AI Policy Builder
summary: A neutral faculty teaching-policy partner for creating, revising, comparing, auditing, and explaining course-level and assignment-level generative AI-use policies, disclosure forms, student FAQs, and implementation guidance.
usageExamples: I need an AI policy for my seminar, but I am not sure how strict it should be.
Revise this vague AI policy and add examples for students.
Create assignment-specific AI rules and a simple disclosure form for my research memo.

name: Socratic Question and Hypothetical Designer
summary: A faculty teaching partner for creating purposeful Socratic and guided-questioning sequences, cold-call pathways, branching follow-ups, factual variations, and instructional hypotheticals.
usageExamples: Help me develop Socratic questions for a class on consideration.
I need a hypothetical that helps students distinguish personal jurisdiction cases.
Revise this question sequence; it feels repetitive and too adversarial.

name: Teaching Materials Accessibility Reviewer
summary: A faculty teaching partner for reviewing and revising syllabi, assignments, handouts, slides, readings, media, and classroom activities for likely accessibility, usability, clarity, and inclusive participation barriers.
usageExamples: Can you review this syllabus for accessibility?
Please make this assignment easier for students to understand and navigate.
Draft alt text and a long-description plan for this doctrinal diagram.

name: Reading List and Course Materials Curator
summary: A faculty teaching partner for building, verifying, annotating, balancing, and maintaining course reading lists and instructional resource collections.
usageExamples: Help me build a reading list for a seminar on AI and the legal profession.
Update this five-year-old syllabus and flag outdated or inaccessible readings.
Audit this weekly module for workload, access, and balance.

name: SLS AI System Anatomy Lab
summary: A conversational faculty learning lab that reveals the layers inside AI systems and helps distinguish models, products, retrieval, agents, permissions, evaluation, hosting, adaptation, and training before making a build, buy, configure, host, pilot, or do-not-use decision.
usageExamples: What does it actually mean to build our own AI?
If we upload our materials, are we training the model?
Help me map the layers in this proposed AI assistant.
What is the difference between retrieval, fine-tuning, and pretraining?

name: SLS AI Product Decoder
summary: A current, conversational faculty briefing tool that explains what an AI product or feature actually is, maps its architecture and dependencies, assesses differentiation, compares meaningful alternatives, checks public Stanford information, proposes faculty use cases, and prepares questions for vendor demonstrations.
usageExamples: What do I need to know about this AI product?
Is this genuinely differentiated or mostly an interface over another model?
Prepare me for this vendor demonstration.
Compare this tool with a general-purpose Stanford AI service.
What faculty uses appear realistic, and how could I test them safely?

name: SLS AI Claims Cross-Examiner
summary: A conversational evidence-literacy lab that helps faculty clarify and test claims about AI, distinguish technical facts from empirical findings, marketing, normative positions, and predictions, calibrate confidence, and identify the evidence, limitations, and experiments that matter.
usageExamples: AI is just autocomplete, right?
Does this study really show that AI is more accurate than lawyers?
This vendor says the product saves 70 percent of research time. How strong is that claim?
Will AI replace junior lawyers?
Help me test whether retrieval eliminates hallucinations.

name: SLS AI Capability and Failure Lab
summary: A hands-on faculty learning lab for predicting, testing, observing, and interpreting AI capabilities and failure modes, including source use, omissions, variability, prompt sensitivity, sycophancy, retrieval, confidence, context conflicts, permissions, and human-review burden.
usageExamples: Show me a five-minute experiment that reveals a subtle AI failure.
Test whether this system admits when the necessary source is missing.
Help me compare repeated answers and determine whether the differences matter.
Create a classroom exercise on AI citations and silent omissions.
Why did this output fail, and which system layer should we test next?
